title: Why Decision Trees Overfit So Easily
concept: Without rules to stop them, decision trees can keep asking questions until they memorize every individual training point, creating hyper-specific branches that fail on new data.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary way a decision tree makes classification predictions?

options.text: By asking a series of yes/no questions to route data down different branches.
By calculating the mathematical average of all features at once.
By finding the widest straight margin between two classes.

exercises.explain: Correct! Decision trees act like a flow chart, asking questions step-by-step to reach a conclusion. But if they ask too many questions, they can run into trouble!

cards.title: The Memorized Exam
cards.body: Imagine studying for a history exam by memorizing the exact wording of the practice questions: 'On July 4, 1776, the declaration was signed.' On exam day, the question asks: 'When was the US declaration of independence signed?' Because the phrasing is slightly different, you freeze and fail. You memorized the exact examples rather than the actual concept!

cards.title: Overfitting in Trees
cards.body: Overfitting occurs when a decision tree grows without limits. It keeps splitting data into smaller and smaller branches until it has memorized the exact training points, including their noise and random quirks, instead of finding general, useful rules.

cards.title: Too Many Labels
cards.body: Imagine sorting your clothes. Storing them in broad boxes like 'Shirts' or 'Pants' is simple and works for new clothes. But if you create a box labeled 'Blue cotton shirts with a stain on the left sleeve purchased on a rainy Tuesday,' it's too specific. You've created a box for just one item, which won't help you sort future shirts.

cards.title: The Hyper-Specific House
cards.body: A model tries to predict house prices. A simple tree might learn: 'Houses with more than 3 bedrooms are expensive.' An unrestricted tree keeps splitting until it has a branch for a single house: 'A house with a red door, a scratched mailbox, and 4 bedrooms is worth exactly $423,500.' That rule is useless for any other house.

cards.title: Deeper Trees Aren't Better
cards.body: You might think a decision tree with 100 levels of questions is highly accurate and smart. In reality, deep trees are usually much worse on new data because they've built rules around random coincidences in their training sets.

cards.title: General vs. Memorized Rules
cards.body: Compare how simple rules generalize, while hyper-specific rules overfit:

left.text:
Red fruit + Round shape = Apple.
Simple, robust, and works for almost any new apple you find.

right.text:
Red fruit + Round + Exactly 3 spots + Picked on a Thursday = Apple.
Fails for a clean apple picked on Friday.

exercises.prompt: What happens when a decision tree is allowed to split without any restrictions?

options.text: It keeps creating branches until it has memorized individual training points, leading to overfitting.
It automatically deletes all questions and guesses the average value.
It simplifies its rules to ensure they work perfectly on all future data.
It converts itself into a single straight boundary line.

exercises.explain: Correct! Without limits, a decision tree will split repeatedly to fit the training data perfectly, memorizing individual points and overfitting.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A decision tree that achieves 100% accuracy on its training data is guaranteed to make perfect predictions on new, unseen data.
exercises.explain: False! High training accuracy often means the tree has overfit by memorizing the training data. It will likely struggle on new, unseen data.

exercises.sentence: When a decision tree creates hyper-specific rules to match the training data perfectly, it is ___.
exercises.explain: Overfitting means the model has learned the training data too well, including its noise and random details.

exercises.prompt: Which of these decision tree rules shows signs of overfitting?

options.text: If customer age is over 18 AND favorite color is blue AND zip code is 90210 AND they signed up on a rainy morning -> Recommend product A
If customer age is over 18 -> Recommend product A
If customer age is under 18 AND they have a subscription -> Recommend product B

exercises.explain: Exactly! The first rule is extremely specific and relies on coincidences (favorite color is blue, rainy morning), which is a classic sign of overfitting.

exercises.prompt: You build a decision tree to identify toxic mushrooms. The model achieves 100% accuracy on your collection. However, when you test it on new mushrooms from the forest, it incorrectly classifies a toxic one as safe because it had a slightly different shade of brown. What went wrong?

options.text: The tree overfit by splitting on highly specific details of your training collection, rather than learning general toxic indicators.
The tree was too simple and failed to ask enough questions about the mushrooms.
The mushrooms changed their toxicity level because of the weather.
The tree was pruned too early and did not have enough branches.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The tree overfit to your specific collection, building hyper-specific rules about precise shades of brown. Since it didn't focus on general indicators, it failed on new mushrooms. Next, let's learn how to trim these overly specific branches!
